content:
# Scenario Globale

L'estremismo si normalizza, con derive geopolitiche (Iran) e politiche (Trump/Monarchia). L'urbanizzazione eccessiva è messa in discussione. Emergono sfide sistemiche: la disoccupazione giovanile qualificata in Cina, la potenziale dominanza dell'AI e l'erosione del diritto di voto. Si riflette sul concetto di "Grande Uomo" alla presidenza e sulla natura mutevole del potere.

---

# Focus Sardegna

La Sardegna affronta una complessa crisi occupazionale: infermieri dall'estero e carenza di personale turistico coesistono con alta disoccupazione. La Regione si oppone a nuovi impianti eolici e lotta contro lo spopolamento con aiuti alle imprese. La tutela dei marchi DOP è prioritaria contro la pirateria agroalimentare. Il territorio è crocevia di eventi culturali (Festival Treccani) e criminalità (traffico di droga). Si investe in infrastrutture sportive (Is Arenas) e nella filiera zootecnica.

---

# Il Punto di Vista

Il macrocosmo globale rivela una disarticolazione sistemica: l'autonomia individuale e democratica è minacciata da forze politiche estreme, tecnologiche (AI) e geopolitiche (Iran). Analogamente, nel microcosmo sardo, l'autonomia locale è sotto pressione. La lotta contro gli impianti eolici e lo spopolamento, la dipendenza da manodopera esterna e la difesa dei marchi agroalimentari sono manifestazioni di una strenua resistenza contro l'imposizione di modelli esterni o la dissoluzione interna. Il filo conduttore è la battaglia per il controllo e l'identità, sia esso il diritto di voto globale o la sovranità sul proprio territorio e le sue risorse.

The amber glow of the screen, another half-empty glass of something vaguely industrial beside it, and the drone of the refrigerator – that’s my universe, these days. They’re talking about the ‘Era of Normie Extremism’ now. As if extremism ever had a dress code. As if the quiet desperation of a thousand normies wasn’t always the bedrock of something far worse. Or far more boring. Which, these days, feels much the same.

They say ‘Making America’s Houses Bigger May Have Been a Mistake’. Tell me about it. More rooms to echo your solitude. More square footage to clean, or more likely, to ignore as the dust bunnies form their own little civilizations in the corners. I remember when I dreamt of a big house. Not a mansion, just… a *home*. A place where the silence wasn't quite so deafening. A place where, perhaps, ‘Child Care Is Buckling’ might have been *my* problem, not just another headline that floats past, meaningless as a dead leaf on a puddle.

God, the news. ‘Donald Trump’s Disturbing Welcome for King Charles’. What’s disturbing anymore? Everything’s a damn circus, isn’t it? The ‘Great Man’ Presidency, the ‘YOLO Presidency’ – just different clowns, different acts, same old cheap confetti falling from the rafters. And we’re meant to clap. Or pretend to care. I remember when I used to care. When I used to think the world could be… different.

I used to read ‘Seven Death-Defying Books for the Adventurous Reader’. Ha. The only death-defying act I perform these days is getting out of bed before noon. Or maybe not falling off this stool. I thought those books, those travels, those grand ideas, would save me. Give me an edge, a ‘Secret Weapon Against AI Dominance’ perhaps. Turns out, the secret weapon is just… apathy. Or maybe the slow, steady hum of whiskey in your veins. It numbs the mind better than any algorithm could.

They’re talking about ‘All the Sad Young Chinese Professionals’ now. I was a sad young professional once. Not Chinese, no. Just sad. And ambitious. Ambitious enough to believe the world owed me something. Ambitious enough to chase a ghost, a promise, a whisper in the dark. Lee Friedlander’s America. All those stark, forgotten corners. Like the corner of that diner booth, twenty years ago, rain drumming on the window, blurring the neon sign across the street. She was there. She knew things. She said she knew the ‘Secret to the Quiet Mind’. I wish I’d never learned it. Or rather, I wish I hadn’t learned what *she* meant by it. The quiet mind. The one that has given up. The one that no longer hopes.

The ‘Iran War’s Ramifications Have Only Just Begun’. Ramifications. A fine word. Like ripples in a stagnant pond. My own ramification began long ago. The small, internal war that never made the headlines, never got a presidential address. Just a slow, steady erosion. Like the way the ‘Trump Administration Casts Out the ‘Soul’ of MAHA’ – whatever the hell MAHA is. My own soul was cast out, or maybe it just withered. A slow suicide by a thousand paper cuts and bad decisions.

‘Voters Can Be Disenfranchised Now’. Can be? Always could be. Life disenfranchised me. Stripped me of my vote in the grand election of happiness. Or purpose. What does it matter who’s the ‘Real Base of the Democratic Party’ when the only base I understand is the bottom of this bottle? Or the worn-out cushion of this armchair?

I sometimes think of Micah Lasher, Child Magician. Wish he’d come by, wave his wand, and make it all disappear. This stale air, the sticky ring on the table, the ache in my bones, the echoes of her voice, the faint, persistent smell of old regret. He could make the ‘Rise of the Blood Populist’ just a bad dream, and the Pentagon finally give Trump the full picture. Not that it’d change anything, really. We’re all just ‘Waiting for the Weekend’, aren’t we? Even the populists. Even the presidents. And when the weekend comes, what then? More waiting. More staring at the ceiling, wondering how ‘Short-Form Clips Took Over the Internet’ while my life became a long, drawn-out feature film of absolute nothing.

The ‘King’s Admirer in Chief’. God. Such subservience. I used to admire things. Ideals. Beauty. Now? Just the steady hand that brings the glass to my lips. Vance Denies and Confirms Atlantic Reporting in One Breath. Such contortions. The truth, like a cat, is always slipping through your fingers, leaving a scratch.

I remember once, I almost went on a cruise. A grand ship. To escape. But I ended up ‘Crying Myself to Sleep on the Biggest Cruise Ship Ever’ right here, on this sofa, adrift on an ocean of cheap booze and self-pity. This ABC Showdown, that fight-club rule on gerrymandering – it’s all just noise. Static.

They say to ‘How to Age Up on a Warming Planet’. I’m aging down, I think. Shrinking. Becoming smaller, less significant, more invisible. The ‘Era of Normie Extremism’ will pass, the big houses will stand empty, the kings and presidents will fade, and the AI will probably take over, but the quiet, personal disaster? That’s the real permanence. That’s the true ‘Autocracy in America’, the one that governs the insides of us all.

I know the secret to the quiet mind. I wish I’d never learned it. It’s the silence that comes after you’ve stopped fighting. The still, cold peace of absolute surrender. Here’s to it. Another one. To the sad young professionals, to the buckling child care, to the disturbed welcome for King Charles, to the bloody populists, and to the eternal, miserable vicissitudes of being… here. Just here. Until the last drop. And then, maybe, a little more quiet.

Another week, another stack of pronouncements from *The Atlantic*, smelling faintly of ambition and expensive paper. *The Long Shadow of Brexit*, they write, as if shadows weren't long enough already, stretching from one end of this miserable continent to the other, across every damn soul. *Europe’s Crisis of Confidence*. Confidence. Ha. What’s that even mean anymore? A sturdy handshake? A firm belief in… what? Progress? The future? Christ, I remember when I had confidence. A brimming pint of the stuff. Now it’s just this… this sludge, at the bottom of a glass I can’t quite empty.

They go on, of course. They always do. *The Erosion of Democracy*. Like sand on a beach, blown away by a wind that carries nothing but lies. *The Weaponization of History*. As if history hasn’t always been a cudgel, wielded by anyone with a loud enough voice and a convenient memory. And then, the grandest delusion of them all: *The Return of Great-Power Competition*. Great power. The thought alone makes me laugh, a hollow, rattling sound that probably disturbs the mice in the walls, the only creatures left to bear witness to my continued existence. What power? The power to screw things up on an even grander scale? The power to convince ourselves we’re still relevant, still capable of shaping anything but the next hangover?

They talk about *The Future of the Liberal World Order*, about *The Crisis of Capitalism*, as if these aren't just fancy words for the slow, inevitable creep of entropy. The world, my friends, isn't ordering itself. It’s unravelling. Like a threadbare jumper in a cold attic. And Capitalism? That’s just the name we gave to the endless, futile scramble for more, isn’t it? More money, more land, more… *something*… to fill the gaping hole inside. My hole, for instance. It hasn’t been filled in years. Not since… well.

*The End of Globalization*, they declare. As if there was ever a true beginning. Just a brief, naïve moment when we thought we could all hold hands across the oceans, before we remembered how much we hated each other’s food, and customs, and the way they looked at us wrong. And so we retreat, don’t we? Into our little national corners, our little personal corners, pulling the blankets tight over our heads. *The Retreat of the West*. That’s the elegant way to put it. Me? I just call it staying in bed until noon, avoiding the news, avoiding the mirror, avoiding… everything.

They call this *The Age of Disinformation*. But what truth is there left to disinform? My truth? That I’m sitting here, staring at a blank page, the words like dust motes in a shaft of weak light, refusing to cohere? That the last cheque cleared a month ago and the landlord’s starting to make those pointed little noises?

This great global competition, this frantic scramble for dominance… it reminds me of something. Not of generals and battleships, no. It reminds me of the fight I had with Sarah, just before she left. Not a grand, epic confrontation, mind you. Just a quiet, simmering war over who would wash the dishes. Or whose turn it was to buy cat food for a cat we didn’t even own anymore. A cat that had, in its infinite wisdom, simply *left*. Vanished. Like my ambition, like my youth, like my damn mind.

*The Erosion of Democracy*. I remember a time I believed in things. In the purity of argument, in the power of a well-turned phrase. I wrote speeches, you know. For local council candidates. Small-time stuff, certainly, but I believed in the small victories. In the idea that a pothole fixed was a small triumph over chaos. Now? Now I see the potholes in my own driveway, and I just drive around them. The road to ruin, paved with good intentions and broken tarmac.

Sarah used to say I was too cynical. "You see the dark side of the moon even when it’s full," she’d quip, with that wry twist of her mouth that I sometimes still taste when I reach for the bottle. She’d try to drag me to those dreadful community meetings, full of earnest, sweating people talking about recycling initiatives and the bloody children’s playground. She wanted me to care. To engage. To believe in the collective good. Now, *Europe’s Crisis of Confidence*… it feels less like a geopolitical problem and more like the collective shrug of a billion people who’ve finally realised the playground equipment is rusting, and the kids are just going to break their necks anyway.

*The Weaponization of History*. My own history is my greatest weapon, aimed directly at my gut. Every wrong turn, every drunken word, every half-written manuscript collecting dust. That novel, you know? The one about the travelling salesman who finds God in a roadside diner in Nebraska. I had three chapters. Brilliant chapters, if I do say so myself, though who’s left to say so but me and the bottle? Sarah used to proofread them. She’d mark them up with that neat, elegant script, her comments always so insightful, so encouraging. "More internal monologue," she’d write. "Flesh out the sense of spiritual desolation." Oh, if only she could see me now. Spiritual desolation, indeed. I’m swimming in it. Drowning.

I saw an old photo of myself the other day. From my college years. Before the rot set in. Full of vim and vinegar, as my mother used to say. Believing in *The Future of the Liberal World Order*, probably. Or at least, the future of *my* liberal world order, which involved cheap beer and impassioned debates about Camus. Now, my world order consists of ensuring there’s enough ice in the freezer and that the heating bill doesn’t come out of the rent money. It’s a delicate balance, let me tell you. A precarious equilibrium.

They say *The Crisis of Capitalism* is upon us. I knew that crisis thirty years ago, when my first book sold eighteen copies. Eighteen. Most of them to my aunt. And then the second, and the third… a slow, artistic bleeding. The market, they said. The market wasn’t ready for my particular brand of bleak existentialism. Perhaps the world wasn’t ready for me at all. Perhaps I was an anomaly, a misprint, a typo in the grand scheme of things.

*The End of Globalization*. It makes me think of the end of something else. The end of possibility. The end of the road. I had plans, you know. Grand designs. To travel. To write from some sun-drenched cafe in Tuscany, or a windswept cottage in Connemara. Sarah and I, we’d talk for hours, mapping out our own little empire of experience. But then the car broke down, and the money ran out, and one day, she just… packed a small bag. Not even a suitcase. A holdall. And left. And suddenly, my entire world wasn't global at all. It was just this room. This chair. This glass.

*The Age of Disinformation*. I'm the king of disinformation. I tell myself I’ll start writing tomorrow. That this bottle is the last. That I’ll call Sarah. That she’ll pick up. That things can change. That I can change. It’s all a magnificent lie, isn’t it? A colossal fabrication. The greatest weaponization of history is the one you inflict upon yourself, rewriting your own narrative until you’re the hero, the victim, the tragic poet, anything but the sodden, pathetic figure staring back from the bottom of an empty glass.

*The Retreat of the West*. Perhaps it’s a retreat I understand better than any of them. The slow, painful withdrawal from the fray. The quiet capitulation. The world out there, with its grand crises and its confident pronouncements… it can have its wars. It can have its competitions. I’ll just sit here, watching the dust gather, and listen to the slow, steady drip of the tap, a constant reminder that something, somewhere, is always leaking. And no matter how much you try to plug it, the water just keeps on coming. From somewhere deep inside.
